title: Eschatology: The Nature and Identity of the Church — Part 3
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: Lesson 8 on Eschatology_ The Nature and Identity of the Church - Part 3.txt

outline.title: Review: One People of God — No Replacement Theology
subpoints: Previous lessons: the Abrahamic covenant results in one people of God — called Israel in the OT and the church in the NT
This is not replacement theology — it is the same entity; nothing has been replaced
Dispensationalism's charge of 'replacement theology' is a strawman; God does not have two brides
God through Christ has one wife, one bride throughout the history of redemption
The church is Israel; Israel is the church — one continuous entity under different covenant administrations

outline.title: Ephesians 2 — Gentile Christians Made Citizens of Israel
subpoints: Ephesians 2:11-12: Gentiles before Christ were 'aliens from the commonwealth of Israel, strangers from the covenants of promise, having no hope and without God in the world'
Being outside of Israel meant: no access to God's Word, no ordinances or means of grace, not part of the covenant promises, no hope of the Messiah
Ephesians 2:13: 'But now in Christ Jesus you who once were far off have been brought near by the blood of Christ' — near to the commonwealth of Israel
Ephesians 2:19: 'You are no longer strangers and foreigners, but fellow citizens with the saints and members of the household of God'
The only commonwealth mentioned in the whole passage is Israel; to be brought near means to be brought into citizenship of Israel
Paul makes a similar argument in Romans 11 using the olive tree metaphor — Gentiles are grafted into the same olive tree, not a new one; the roots are the promises to Abraham

outline.title: True Israel Has Always Been Defined by Circumcised Hearts
subpoints: The nation of Israel and the church of Israel are not identical even in the OT — lepers were citizens of the nation but excluded from religious life
Deuteronomy 30:6: God promised to circumcise the hearts of His people — heart circumcision has always been the mark of true Israel
Paul in Ephesians 2 calls those circumcised only 'in the flesh by hands' — implying that the Ephesian Gentile Christians are more truly Israel because their hearts are circumcised by the Spirit
God has always been after a people with circumcised hearts — this is the unifying truth across all covenant administrations
The practical implication: we are brought to God in order to be part of a people; salvation is not received in isolation but within a covenant community
1 John: you cannot love God without loving your brothers — union with God is experienced through union with God's people

outline.title: The New Covenant Is the Abrahamic Covenant Fulfilled
subpoints: The new covenant was made with 'the house of Israel and the house of Judah' — Jeremiah 31:31
To benefit from the new covenant, one must be part of the Israel with whom it was made
New covenant theology (Carson, Gentry, et al.) attempts to separate the NT's 'new covenant' from Jeremiah 31 — arguing it is an entirely new covenant unknown before
But Hebrews 8:7-12 is the longest continuous quotation of the OT in the entire NT — it quotes Jeremiah 31 and applies it as the basis of all Christian salvation
Hebrews 9:15: Christ is the mediator of the new covenant, that those called may receive the promised eternal inheritance — referring to Jeremiah's covenant
Hebrews 13:20: the 'everlasting covenant' through Christ's blood = the new covenant of Jeremiah 31
The Lord's Supper instituted with the words 'this cup is the new covenant in my blood' (1 Corinthians 11:25) — sealing Jeremiah's covenant

outline.title: Practical and Prophetic Implications
subpoints: The land promises made to Abraham are also made to the church — Jesus in Matthew 5:5 expands the land promise to 'the earth'
Hebrews 11:13: Abraham died without seeing the promises, meaning they must be realized at the resurrection — Abraham himself will inherit the land at his resurrection
Whether God works a special revival among ethnic Israel in the future is allowed by this view (Romans 9-11) but not required by it
If ethnic Israel is saved in the future, it will be through faith in Jesus Christ — not through a rebuilt temple or re-instituted sacrifices
God has one bride: Israel, the church of the living God — saints before the cross and saints after are the same bride of Christ
Two minutes summary: God is our God and we are His people — the Emmanuel promise applies to us and our children

summary:
In this third installment on the nature and identity of the church, Pastor Tito Lyro brings the doctrinal argument to its conclusion. He begins by dispelling the charge of 'replacement theology': the claim is not that the church replaced Israel, but that Israel and the church are one continuous entity. God does not have two brides. God through Christ has one wife, one bride throughout redemptive history — the Israel of God.

The centerpiece of the lesson is Ephesians 2:11-19, where Paul describes the condition of Gentiles before Christ: aliens from the commonwealth of Israel, strangers from the covenants of promise, without hope and without God in the world. These are not merely political metaphors — being outside of Israel meant no access to the Word of God, no ordinances or means of grace, no covenant promises, and no hope of the Messiah. But now, through the blood of Christ, Gentile believers have been brought near — into citizenship in Israel (Ephesians 2:19). Paul reinforces this with the olive tree image in Romans 11: Gentile believers are grafted into the same olive tree whose roots are the Abrahamic promises, not planted in a new separate tree.

The pastor argues that even in the Old Testament, the nation of Israel and the church of Israel were not identical — lepers were national citizens but excluded from the religious assembly. True Israel has always been defined by circumcised hearts (Deuteronomy 30:6). Paul in Ephesians 2 pointedly calls the circumcised Jews those circumcised 'in the flesh by hands,' implying that Gentile Christians whose hearts are circumcised by the Spirit are actually more truly Israel.

The lesson concludes by anchoring the new covenant in Jeremiah 31 through the argument of Hebrews 8, the longest continuous quotation of the OT in the NT. The new covenant was made with the house of Israel and the house of Judah — to benefit from it, one must be part of that Israel. This settles the question: the church is Israel and Israel is the church, one people through all ages. The practical implication for eschatology: Old Testament prophecies to Israel find their fulfillment in the church, not in a future ethnically separate Jewish kingdom.
transcript:
## Introduction and Review

We have been studying what we call eschatology, the doctrine of the last things. And we've seen so far that this doctrine doesn't just cover things that are so far in the future, but really that's the area of theology that encompasses everything that happens from the moment you and I die to forever. So it's a large area of study. And we've looked at what happens at death, we've looked at hell, we've looked at heaven, we've looked at how people exist between the moment of their death and the moment of the resurrection. And we have then started looking at the church and Israel as it relates to their connection. And as it relates to these ideas, these teachings of things that are future to us and the fulfillment of prophecy.

The reason we're spending so much time on the nature and identity of the church is that it is of the utmost importance to know what the church is and how it relates to Israel in figuring out how prophecies from the Old Testament will be fulfilled in the time future to us. We've seen that there are several ways to look at that identity of the church. The two primary ones are: the church and Israel are completely different institutions — they do not have anything in common with one another, and God has a plan for Israel as an ethnic people and a plan for the church as a spiritual people. That falls under this theology called dispensationalism, which has this sharp distinction between the church and Israel.

We saw that historically that is a minority position even though today in the United States it may be the majority position. Historically that's not the case. It's this idea that there's this sharp distinction between the church and Israel is the new kid on the block — it's about 120, 150 years old and it was developed in England, first by a man with the last name of Darby. But it was popularized and became famous in the United States especially in the late 1800s in the prophecy conference movement. And that became so strong, so powerful, so ingrained in American culture that it has actually driven American foreign policy.

So far we have seen several things in this particular subdivision of our study. We've seen that the word church is used in several ways in the Bible. We also saw that the argument that the English word church is not used in the Old Testament and therefore the church wasn't there is not an actual intelligent argument based on linguistics and how the Bible is written. We saw that the concept of the church is there throughout the Old Testament. And we saw that God established his church when he entered into a covenant with Abraham.

## One People of God — Not Replacement Theology

I'm going to start today by looking at the fact that the Abrahamic covenant results in one people of God, commonly called Israel in the Old Testament and the church in the New Testament. And then our dispensational brothers are going to say, oh, you're going to replace Israel with the church, you believe replacement theology. No. It's the same entity. So nothing's been replaced. We're not saying that God unplugged this and plugged something else in. No. It's the same entity. So there's no replacement. It's the same bride of Christ from beginning to end. God through Christ is not a bigamist. He didn't have a couple of wives. It's one wife, one bride throughout the history of redemption.

And actually we're going to anchor that in one particular passage if you grab a Bible and go to Ephesians chapter 2. Ephesians chapter 2. And I want to show to you from here that when you come to faith in Jesus Christ, you become a citizen of the Israel of God.

Now you might say, but in the New Testament, the nation of Israel and the religious people of Israel are the same. And one — to be a member of the nation of Israel, you're a member of the church of Israel. That's not true. We can think of examples of people who are national Israelites who are not members of the church of Israel. Lepers were citizens of the nation of Israel but they were not members of the church of Israel. They were forbidden from being members of the church of Israel. So let's be careful that even though there's a lot of overlap, the nation of Israel and the church of Israel are not the exact same thing in the Old Testament.

## Ephesians 2 — Gentile Christians Made Citizens of Israel

But what I want to see here in Ephesians 2 is that one cannot be a Christian if one is not part of Israel. Look at verse 11 of chapter 2. Therefore, remember that you once were Gentiles in the flesh, who are called 'the uncircumcision' by what is called 'the circumcision' made in the flesh by hands, that at that time you were without Christ, being aliens from the commonwealth of Israel, and strangers from the covenants of promise, having no hope and without God in the world.

But now, in Christ Jesus, you who once were far off have been brought near by the blood of Christ. And then drop to verse 19. Now, therefore, you are no longer strangers and foreigners, but fellow citizens with the saints and members of the household of God, having been built on the foundation of the apostles and prophets, Jesus Christ himself being the chief cornerstone, in whom the whole building, being fitted together, grows into a holy temple in the Lord, in whom you also are being built together for a dwelling place of God in the Spirit.

Now, what does it mean to be a foreigner to Israel? What would somebody who was not part of Israel and in the Old Testament experience? Well, Israel is the place where people met with God. You really didn't meet in a corporate visible way with God outside of Israel. Israel is the place where the Word of God was available. Romans 3 tells us that Israel was the stewards of the oracles of God. God spoke to the nations, and if the nations of the world wanted to hear from God, they were to come to Israel. Israel was also the place where all the ordinances and all the means of grace were available — you couldn't do sacrifices anywhere you wanted; they had to be on the altar in the tabernacle or temple. So you can't really meet with God in that way anywhere else.

Israel was the object of the promises of God's covenants. So if you want to be part of those covenants, you have to be in Israel. If you're not in connection with Israel, then you're not part of any of that. Israel was united with God, so to not be part of Israel is to not be united with God. To be part of Israel meant being chosen by God, and it meant you had the hope of the promised Messiah. So to be a foreigner to Israel means that you don't have any of these things.

And that's exactly the picture that Paul is painting of those who are outside of Christ in Ephesians chapter 2. So you can see here that Paul equates the impact of being outside of Christ with the same impact of being outside of Israel. You don't have these benefits.

The Ephesian church, being mostly Gentiles, had experienced the taunting and putting down from the Jews. The Jews would tell them, 'You're not a real child of God. You're not circumcised.' And Paul makes a bit of a dig on the Jews when he says that they are circumcised only 'in the flesh by hands' — instead of having the heart circumcised by the Spirit. True citizens of Israel actually have their hearts circumcised by the Spirit of God. And Paul tells the Ephesians here that they are more citizens of Israel than those circumcised in the flesh, because the blood of Christ is upon them.

And Paul, in a different place, uses the same idea, though with a different imagery. In Romans 11, he uses the imagery of a tree. When you come to faith in Jesus Christ, you are grafted into the same olive tree — not a new olive tree. And as you read the argument of chapter 11, the roots of that olive tree are the promises made to Abraham in the Abrahamic covenant. That's where the tree sprouts up, and we are grafted into the very same tree as Old Testament Israel.

So when people call it 'the church age,' as if God was doing a new program, or something that was hidden before — no. It's continued the same program with some changes, but the same body, the same program, the same tree, as He had going on through the whole Old Testament, starting with Abraham himself.

And remember, Abraham was not a Jew — there's something that we forget. Abraham was a Gentile. And the history of Israel starts with a Gentile being brought to faith. And Jesus says Abraham knew of me and he knew of my day and he rejoiced in it. Romans 4: the gospel that's been preached to us was also preached to Abraham, and he believed it.

## True Israel Is Defined by Circumcised Hearts

So if all that is true then, through faith in Christ, you are brought near to Israel and become citizens of Israel. And that has been always what made somebody part of the true Israel of God. God has always been after that, and those have always been his people, who have their hearts circumcised. Even way back in Deuteronomy, where they're about to enter the Promised Land, God says in Deuteronomy 30:6: the Lord your God will circumcise your heart and the heart of your offspring, that you love the Lord your God with all your heart, with all your soul, that you may live. That has always been the goal. Circumcised hearts, so that one is part of the true Israel of God.

On the practical level, it is amazing to me how Paul speaks of no longer being separated from God by being united to a people. Look at verse 13 again. But now in Christ Jesus, you who once were far off have been brought near by the blood of Christ. You are now brought in fellowship with God by being united to a people. God is not just after individuals getting saved. That's not what the Great Commission teaches. What God is after is people being born again, coming to faith in Christ, to grow together as a sample of Christ in a body. God is not just after people getting saved — He is making them part of a people, part of a community, part of a family.

Your union with God is experienced through your union with God's people. 1 John is very clear on that — John says, you can say that you love God till you're blue in the face, but if you hate your brother, you're no more than a liar. You cannot experience God apart from the people in his body as a believer.

## The New Covenant Is the Abrahamic Covenant Fulfilled

The next thing I want us to see is that the new covenant is a further development of the Abrahamic covenant. It was made with Israel. Every Lord's Day we have the Lord's Supper, and when we read 1 Corinthians 11, Paul gives us the earliest account of the institution of the Lord's Supper. And remember what Jesus says: this cup is the new covenant in my blood. So if we are going to be in the right relationship with God, we need to be part of the new covenant.

And the new covenant is a development of the Abrahamic covenant. If we are not part of Israel, we have no part in the new covenant. How can I say that? Well, read what the new covenant says. Jeremiah 31 is where it is actually labeled 'new covenant,' and this is what God says: behold, the days are coming, declares the Lord, when I will make a new covenant. Now, with whom? With the house of Israel and the house of Judah. Those are the entities. That's the completion of Israel — the northern and southern kingdoms together. So to benefit from the new covenant, you have to be part of that.

Some might try to get around this necessity of being a member of Israel in order to participate in the promises of the new covenant by saying that when the New Testament talks about the new covenant, it's talking about something else than the new covenant in Jeremiah 31. Some say that the New Testament's 'new covenant' is a covenant never mentioned before — therefore the church has nothing to do with Israel. D.A. Carson and others have said this. That's a major problem though, and that problem is called the Book of Hebrews.

Would you turn to Hebrews chapter 8 for a second? This is a visual illustration. Hebrews 8, starting at verse 7 — if your Bible has headings, you probably have a heading: 'A New Covenant.' Now starting at verse 7, all the way to verse 12, what is that the author of Hebrews, the Holy Spirit, is quoting when he talks about the new covenant? It's Jeremiah 31. This is the longest contiguous quotation of the Old Testament in the entire Bible. Jeremiah 31 is quoted here. And then the author develops that in verses 8 through 11, saying that this is the basis of our salvation. And in chapter 11, he says this was the basis of the salvation of all these people who were saved by faith in the Old Testament.

And Hebrews 9:15: Jesus is the mediator of the new covenant, to those who are called, that they may receive the promised eternal inheritance, since a death has occurred that redeems them from the transgressions committed under the first covenant. So unless you're part of this new covenant — and which new covenant? The one promised to Israel — unless you're part of that covenant, unless you're part of that people with whom the covenant was made, you have no salvation.

And Hebrews 13:20 calls it the 'everlasting covenant' — this is the same covenant, made through the blood of Christ. And 1 Corinthians 11:25 records Jesus saying at the Last Supper: this cup is the new covenant in my blood. So the Lord's Supper seals that very covenant that was promised to Israel in Jeremiah 31.

## Practical and Prophetic Implications

We have a great God that saves even inconsistent people. So we're thankful for that. But theologically, that's why this teaching matters: unless you're a member of the new covenant — the one promised to Israel — you have no part in Christ.

So everyone who comes to faith in Christ is grafted into Israel and becomes a member of the new covenant. Why is it important? Because now we know how properly to understand the prophecies made in the Old and New Testaments regarding Israel and the church, because they are a continuing body.

Sure, there are differences since the different administrations of the covenant with Abraham. We don't sacrifice animals anymore — Hebrews tells us why, because Christ was sacrificed for us. We don't worship in a temple anymore because Christ is our temple and is everywhere, so we can worship Him everywhere. But the gist of being a people to God, and God being our God, is the same from the beginning all the way to eternity as we read in Revelation 21 and 22.

The land promises made to Abraham are also made to the church. We're going to look at that a little later. Jesus in Matthew 5:5 uses land-promise language — the meek shall inherit the earth, not just Palestine. And Hebrews 11:13 says that Abraham died in faith without seeing the promises fulfilled. So those promises still have to be realized — at the resurrection, when Abraham comes back to life, those promises can be realized.

Now whether God will do a work of revival among ethnic Israel is something that this view allows for, but doesn't require. It may be that God still has some revival for ethnic Israel, as some understand Romans 9-11. And this view allows for that. But if God does work a special work of saving ethnic Israel in the future, it will be through faith in Jesus Christ — not through some sort of obedience on their part, but through faith in Jesus Christ. Because as it has always been, reconciliation between man and God only happens by the grace of God through faith in the mediator of the covenant, independent from any of our works.

So our great God has one bride and one bride only. The name of the bride is Israel, the Church of the living God. That's who she is, that's us, that's the saints that lived before the cross, that's the saints that continue to live from the cross to the coming of Jesus Christ.

Any questions before we close? All right, let's pray together. Father in heaven, thank you so much for showing to us that you are a consistent, faithful, unchanging God, and that we can bank on that. We thank you for your promise to be a God to us, and that we are your people. We thank you, Father, that you don't change in that. Because of that, we can confidently come to Christ knowing that He is God with us, and through Him we are saved. We pray to prepare our hearts and minds to worship you this morning, in Jesus' name, Amen.
